coredata.dc:title: Yellow emissive Se,N-codoped carbon dots toward sensitive fluorescence assay of crystal violet
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yaoping
Gao, Zhijin
coredata.dc:description: Crystal violet (CV), a hazardous dye, poses a serious threat to the environment and human health. This motivates us to develop a facile method for its sensitive detection. Herein, we demonstrate a rapid sensing of CV using a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, yellow emissive Se,N-codoped carbon dots (CDs). CDs with an intense photoluminescence peak at 566 nm are synthesized by a hydrothermal technique using selenourea and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. This material shows a high quantum yield of up to 16.7 %. It is found that the yellow fluorescence of CDs can be selectively quenched by CV, which makes them promising for CV sensing. The linearity is obeyed in the range of 0.02–1.60 μM, and the limit of detection is as low as 7.3 nM. After detailed investigations, the inner filter effect is proposed to be the sensing mechanism. For practical usage, the newly built method is applied to determine the trace amount of CV in fish tissue samples, and satisfactory results are obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crystal violet
Carbon dots
Yellow fluorescence
Sensitive detection
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of CV in fish tissues

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) Raman, and (d) FTIR spectra of the yellow emissive CDs. Inset in (a): HRTEM image of a single dot.
(a) XPS full scan, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) Se3d spectra of CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (b) PLE and PL spectra of CDs. Insets in (a): photographs of the aqueous dispersion of CDs under daylight and a UV lamp (365 nm).

(a) The PL spectra of yellow emissive CDs upon addition of different concentrations of CV (from top to bottom: 0.00, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80, 1.00, 1.30, 1.60, 2.00, 2.50, and 3.00 μM). (b) The linear relationship between F0/F and CV concentration in the range of 0.02–1.60 μM. (c) The fluorescence response of CDs toward various ions. The concentrations of all the ions are 32.00 μM. The CV concentration is 1.60 μM. (d) The fluorescence response of CDs toward several important physiological molecules. The concentrations of all the analytes are 1.60 μM. The CD concentration is 9 mg L–1. The excitation wavelength is 412 nm.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CV, CDs, the mixture of CDs and CV (CDs/CV, experimental), and the physical addition of the spectra of CDs and CV (theoretical). (b) The PL, PLE spectra of CDs, and absorption spectra of CV. (c) The PL decay profiles of CDs before and after addition of CV. (d) The fluorescence response of CDs toward CV at different temperatures.
A diagram about the synthesis of yellow emissive CDs and their application for CV detection.
The results for CV detection in fish samples based on the yellow emissive CDs.

head.ce:title: Yellow emissive Se,N-codoped carbon dots toward sensitive fluorescence assay of crystal violet

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo, 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Crystal violet (CV), a hazardous dye, poses a serious threat to the environment and human health. This motivates us to develop a facile method for its sensitive detection. Herein, we demonstrate a rapid sensing of CV using a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, yellow emissive Se,N-codoped carbon dots (CDs). CDs with an intense photoluminescence peak at 566 nm are synthesized by a hydrothermal technique using selenourea and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. This material shows a high quantum yield of up to 16.7 %. It is found that the yellow fluorescence of CDs can be selectively quenched by CV, which makes them promising for CV sensing. The linearity is obeyed in the range of 0.02–1.60 μM, and the limit of detection is as low as 7.3 nM. After detailed investigations, the inner filter effect is proposed to be the sensing mechanism. For practical usage, the newly built method is applied to determine the trace amount of CV in fish tissue samples, and satisfactory results are obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Crystal violet (CV), a protein dye containing aromatic rings, is widely used in textile, paper, and pharmaceutical industries (Yakout et al., 2019; Siao et al., 2018). Previously, it is also applied as a fungicide in the aquaculture (Marco-Brown et al., 2018). Unfortunately, CV is toxic to organisms and may cause human carcinogenesis and mutagenesis, and thus is banned to use in the aquaculture now (Jiang et al., 2015). Due to its harmful impacts, the sensitive and selective detection of CV is in high demand.

Over the past several years, some methods have been demonstrated for the determination of CV, including immunosorbent assay (Shen et al., 2011), microextraction coupled with high performance liquid chromatography (Zhang et al., 2012), and liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry (Xu et al., 2012). However, these methods suffer from complex instruments, tedious pretreatment steps, and/or long detection time. Fluorescence approaches have advantages of simplicity and rapidness. CV detection based on fluorescent YVO4:Eu3+and silica nanoparticles were realized (Yi, 2017; Han et al., 2018b). Nevertheless, the high cost of YVO4:Eu3+ and the low quantum yield (QY) of silica nanoparticles (0.43 %) are the drawbacks for real applications. Therefore, it is quite necessary to design cheap and efficient fluorescent probes for the accurate analysis of CV.

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging class of nanocarbons with lateral dimensions below 10 nm, promise to advance a wide array of technological applications ranging from sensing, imaging, theranostics to catalysis and energy conversion, due to their unique optical, chemical, and physical properties (Han et al., 2018a; Hu et al., 2019a; Liu et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019). One of the most fascinating features of CDs is photoluminescence (PL). However, a majority of CDs emit short wavelength light that is typically in the blue to green region (Shamsipur et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2017). Yellow or red emissive CDs with PL peaks beyond 550 nm are difficult to obtain, which greatly hampers the extensive utilization of CDs, particularly in the fields of bioimaging, lighting devices, and sensing, where long wavelength emissive CDs are urgently demanded because of their irreplaceable roles (Gao et al., 2018). Recently, enormous efforts have been made to expand the emission of CDs to long wavelength, by selecting proper precursors and/or modifying surface states (Jiang et al., 2015a, 2015b; Wang et al., 2018; Yuan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; Ye et al., 2019). For instance, Lin group synthesized yellow emissive CDs through solvothermal treatment of 1,2,4-triaminobenzene (Jiang et al., 2015b). Wang and co-workers reported on sulfur-doped CDs with yellow emission from durian (Wang et al., 2018). Despite some impressive advances, the facile preparation of long wavelength emissive CDs that have a high QY is still at early stage, and a lot of work needs to be done for the development.

In this paper, we address a hydrothermal synthesis of Se,N-codoped CDs with intense yellow emission using selenourea and o-phenylenediamine as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The as-synthesized CDs achieve a high QY of up to 16.7 % in aqueous solution. We find that the yellow fluorescence of CDs can be specifically suppressed by CV attributing to the inner filter effect (IFE), which renders them a potential CV sensor. To support the practicality, the CD-based quantitative evaluation of CV in the fish tissue sample is further demonstrated.

Selenourea and o-phenylenediamine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company. Crystal violet (CV), leucocrystal violet (LCV), malachite green (MG), leucomalachite green (LMG), glucose (Glu), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), glutathione (GSH), and cysteine (Cys) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Company. Other reagents were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Company. The fishes, namely carp and grouper, were got from the local supermarket in Ningbo City. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

In a typical synthesis, 100 mg of o-phenylenediamine, 50 mg of selenourea, and 30 mL of water were added into a 50-mL autoclave. After 10-minute sonication, the autoclave was transferred to an oven and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. A brown mixture containing yellow emissive CDs was obtained after reaction. For purification of CDs, the mixture passed through a 0.22-μm filter to remove the residue, and then dialyzed against deionized water using a 1000-Da cellulose membrane. The powder of CDs was finally got by freeze-drying.

In brief, 9 mg of the yellow emissive CDs were dispersed in 100 mL of phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4). Then, 2700 μL of the CV solution in a certain concentration was mixed with 300 μL of the above CD dispersion. After 1-minute shaking, the ﬂuorescence spectrum of the mixture was recorded under the excitation wavelength at 412 nm. The selectivity experiments were conducted by replacing CV with other chemicals. For quantitative analysis, the ﬂuorescence intensity at 566 nm was taken.

The fish tissues from carp and grouper were pretreated before use (Han et al., 2018b). The muscles of fishes were minced and frozen. After that, 10 g of the thawed muscles were put into a 50-mL beaker containing 30 mL of acetonitrile and sonicated for 1 h. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged. Anhydrous sodium sulfate was then added to the mixture. After evaporating acetonitrile, the sample was dispersed in phosphate buffer. For analysis of CV, 1200 μL of the as-prepared sample was mixed with 1500 μL of the standard CV solution (0.00, 1.00, 2.00, and 3.00 μM) and 300 μL of the CD dispersion. Other detection procedures were identical as described above.

As seen in the large area TEM image (Fig. 1 a), the as-prepared CDs are nearly uniform and monodisperse with a quasi-spherical shape in the sizes of 1.8–3.8 nm (Fig. 1b). High-resolution (HR) TEM image of a single particle (inset in Fig. 1a) reveals the amorphous nature of CDs, because the diffraction contrast of the nanomaterial is low and no clear lattice fringe is observed. In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c), two peaks at 1355 and 1607 cm–1 can be identified as the D and G bands, respectively. The former is associated with the disordered sp3 carbons, while the latter represents the graphite sp2 ones (Hu et al., 2019b). The coexistence of both two bands with intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.55 demonstrates that many structural defects have formed in CDs. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d) depicts the stretching vibrations of CC/C = N at 1502 cm–1 and out-plane deformations of aromatic CH at 745 cm–1, implying that CDs contain aromatic clusters. Diverse surface functional groups are presented, including the characteristic bands of OH/NH at 3385 and 3159 cm–1, CO at 1628 cm–1, and CO/CSe in the region of 1016–1275 cm–1. The CN bond at 1400 cm–1 is also detected. These FTIR interpretations are further verified by XPS. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) suggests that CDs are composed of C, N, O, and Se elements, with atomic concentrations of 66.71 %, 18.33 %, 11.38 %, and 3.58 % respectively. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b), five carbon species are found: a strong peak of CC/C–C at 284.5 eV that is related to the graphitic domain, and four weaker ones of CN at 285.6 eV, CO at 286.5 eV, CO/CN at 288.4 eV, and C–Se at 290.7 eV (Yang et al., 2015). Deconvolution of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) gives the features of pyridinic N at 398.5 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.5 eV, graphitic N at 400.5 eV, and NH at 400.1 eV (Li et al., 2017), indicating that some nitrogen atoms are incorporated into the carbon framework of CDs, while others are decorated on the surface in the form of NH2. The Se3d spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be fitted into two peaks located at 56.1 and 57.0 eV, which correspond to the signals of CSe and CSeSe, respectively (Yang et al., 2015; Qu et al., 2017). Taken together, the above analysis reveals that the as-synthesized sample is a disordered carbonaceous nanomaterial, doped with Se and N, and surrounded by plentiful groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs exhibits three bands: a shoulder at 235 nm assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 region, two obvious peaks at 291 and 299 nm originated from the n–π* transitions of O/N-containing groups, and a weak band at 425 nm attributed to the low-energy surface state (Sharma et al., 2017). The aqueous dispersion of CDs is yellow in daylight, and emits bright yellow fluorescence under a UV lamp (insets in Fig. 3a). In Fig. 3b, CDs show an excitation-independent PL feature with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 412 and 566 nm, respectively. It is found that the PL excitation (PLE) spectrum overlaps well with the UV–vis absorption curve in the region of 350–600 nm (Fig. S1), indicating that the surface-related absorption is responsible for the yellow fluorescence (Chen et al., 2017). The PL decay dynamic is approximately monoexponential under the excitation of 412 nm (Fig. S2), giving a lifetime of 2.77 ns. Such a monoexponential signal further supports the existence of only one PL center, namely the surface state, accounted for the emission. Using rhodamine 6 G as a reference, the relative QY of CDs in water is measured to be 16.7 %, higher than the most values of reported yellow emissive CDs (Jiang et al., 2015b; Hu et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2017). Over a long-term storage period (Fig. S3), or at high ionic strength (Fig. S4), or under continuous light irradiation (Fig. S5), the PL intensity of CDs remains almost unchanged, demonstrating their excellent emission stability, which is beneficial for many advanced applications.

To utilize their unique optical properties, the as-prepared CDs are proposed to serve as a fluorescent sensor for CV detection. Under the optimal conditions (Figs. S6–S8), a tiny amount of CV (1.00 μM) can remarkably quench the PL of CDs (in a quenching ratio of 49.8 %). Fig. 4 a depicts the PL spectra of CDs upon addition of a series of CV concentrations from 0.00 to 3.00 μM. The PL intensity at 566 nm gradually declines with increased CV concentration, and in the range of 0.02–1.60 μM, a nice linear relationship between F0/F and CV concentration is addressed (Fig. 4b). The equation can be described as F0/F = 0.981 × C CV (μM) + 0.995, with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.998. Based on 3σ/slope, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 7.3 nM, which is nearly 10 times lower than those of formerly reported fluorescence methods (Yi, 2017; Han et al., 2018b). The selectivity of CDs toward CV over other ions and molecules is examined. As shown in Fig. 4c, typical metal cations like Ca2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, and common anions such as Cl–, NO3 –, SO4 2–, PO4 3–, at the concentrations of 20-fold higher than CV, do not interfere with the determination. Some important physiological molecules including glucose (Glu), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), glutathione (GSH), and cysteine (Cys), cause negligible fluorescence quenching under the same conditions. Among the analogues of CV (LCV, LMG and MG), only MG results in a slight interference (Fig. 4d). But this effect is not serious and acceptable in the test. These findings demonstrate the specific fluorescence response of CDs toward CV. Compared with the previous approaches for CV detection (Table S1) (Shen et al., 2011; Yi, 2017; Han et al., 2018b; An et al., 2010; Ghasemi and Kaykhaii, 2016; Huang et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2013), the present method has the combined advantages of simplicity, rapidity, good selectivity, and high sensitivity.

Systematic investigations are carried out to clarify the detection mechanism. Fig. 5 a displays that the experimental UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs/CV mixture nearly coincides with the theoretical one, indicating that the interaction between CDs and CV is weak and no complex generates (Han et al., 2019). This result excludes the quenching mechanism from electron transfer. In Fig. 5b, the absorption band of CV in 450–650 nm has a large overlap with the emission spectrum of CDs, which suggests the possible occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) (Sun and Lei, 2017). Typically, in the FRET process, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor shortens, while for IFE, it remains unchanged (Zhang et al., 2019). It can be seen in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence decay profile of CDs does not change in the presence of CV, and the fluorescence lifetime keeps a constant value. So IFE, rather than FRET, is the main quenching mechanism. Fig. 5d shows that the fluorescence response of CDs toward CV is independent of temperature, which supports IFE (Ling et al., 2014). Moreover, it is found in Fig. 4a that, after adding enough CV, the shape of the fluorescence spectrum alters. This is caused by the fact that, CV, as a fluorescence absorber, has the maximum absorption at 591 nm and insufficient absorption in 650–750 nm, so a new fluorescence peak of the CDs/CV mixture at around 640 nm appears. Such a phenomenon can also be ascribed to IFE (Ling et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CV in fish tissues
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality, CDs are applied to analyze CV in fish tissues. The results are shown in Table 1 . No CV is detected in these edible fish samples. Using the standard addition method, the recoveries vary from 95.3% to 106.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are below 5.8 %, both of which are satisfactory for quantitative assays in real samples and indicate that our fluorescence method has a great application prospect in actual CV detection.

In conclusion, we have introduced a facile approach to prepare yellow emissive Se,N-codoped CDs with a large QY of up to 16.7 %. Based on fluorescence quenching, the as-synthesized CDs can serve as a promising sensor to selectively detect CV in aqueous media. The LOD is determined to be 7.3 nM, much lower than values using the formerly reported fluorescent probes. Substantial evidences indicate that IFE is the sensing mechanism. For practical usage of the newly developed sensor, the assay of CV in fish sample achieves good results. This work provides a feasible way for the synthesis of long wavelength emissive CDs, and extends their applications in toxicant sensing.

Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zhijin Gao: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
